data.title: Pending Home Sales Still In The Deepfreeze, Drop In The West And Midwest, Tick Up In The South And Northeast
data.description:
Originally published on April 21, 2026

Pending home sales in the US overall ticked up in March from February, seasonally adjusted, but were down 1.1% year-over-year and were down by 35% from 2021, by 30% from 2019, and by 31% from 2018. Compared to March 2011, during the Housing Bust and the first March in the data series, pending sales were down by 18%. The housing market is now in the fourth year of this situation.

Regionally, pending sales ticked up from near record lows in the South and the Northeast and fell in the West and Midwest.

In January, national pending sales had dropped to a record low in the data (which goes back to mid-2010) by the National Association of Realtors, and the upticks in February and March are from that record low (historic data via YCharts):

The metric of pending home sales tracks contracts that were signed in March but that haven’t closed yet and could still get canceled because buyers cannot afford homeowner’s insurance or cannot sell their own home, or for other reasons. The rate of cancellations has been running high.

Pending home sales by region.

A map of the four Census Regions is posted in the comments below.

In the West, pending sales fell by 2.6% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, and were just a hair above record lows.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: -1.7% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -4.8%
  • 2023: -3.4%
  • 2022: -36.6%
  • 2021: -41.9%
  • 2019: -37.5%.

In the Midwest, pending sales fell by 1.3% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, and were down 3.1% year-over-year.

December had been the record low in the data going back to mid-2010.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: -3.1% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -3.5%
  • 2023: -4.2%
  • 2022: -21.8%
  • 2021: -25.7%
  • 2019: -24.4%.

In the South, pending sales rose by 3.9% in March from February, seasonally adjusted, with the increases in February and March having not quite reversed the declines in December and January.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: +2.3% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -3.1%
  • 2023: -5.9%
  • 2022: -26.7%
  • 2021: -34.1%
  • 2019: -27.4%.

In the Northeast, pending sales rose by 4.4% in March from February, which had been the second-worst sales on record, behind only lockdown-March 2020. The uptick in March barely made a dent in the prior three months of steep declines. Year-over-year, sales were down 6.6%.

Compared to March in prior years:

  • 2025: -6.6% (year-over-year)
  • 2024: -8.7%
  • 2023: -10.0%
  • 2022: -34.2%
  • 2021: -40.2%
  • 2019: -36.4%.

So in March, the housing resale market remained in the deepfreeze. Sales volume continues to get crushed by too-high home prices that had soared during the years of ultra-low mortgage rates thanks to the Fed’s reckless monetary policies. And it is further hampered by the super-low mortgages still out there that prevent many homeowners from moving into a new home because they cannot afford to, or don’t want to, pay the too-high price to be financed with a regular mortgage rate of around 6% when their current home carries a 3% mortgage.

These too-high home prices and 3% mortgages still out there are depressing home sales, and this has secondary effects on economic activity that home sales generate, such as moving expenses and buying new stuff for the new home, landscaping, remodeling, etc., and these effects can include not taking a new job because it would require a move. This is the so-called “lock-in effect.”

But gradually, life happens, and the lock-in effect loosens just a little every quarter as homeowners pay off their super-low-interest-rate mortgages anyway:

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Broadcom: The Shovel Maker For The Next Wave Of AI
data.description:
Broadcom (AVGO) sits at an almost $1.8 trillion valuation, as the company has reinvented itself into a conglomerate in the tech industry. The company has built a model of acquiring valuable assets, integrating them into its portfolio, and driving strong cash flow. With the growth of AI, Broadcom, as we discussed in our last article, has become integral to the custom silicon that's a key part of the next wave of AI.

Broadcom and Artificial Intelligence

Broadcom has built an impressive artificial intelligence portfolio with impressive and cutting edge packaging solutions.

Broadcom has a wide variety of semiconductor solutions for the AI space, including supporting hyperscale data centers. GPUs are the largest source of expense for data centers, and Nvidia has gross margins above 70%. So it stands to reason that the largest AI capex investors would take a portion of their $100+ billion budgets to build their own custom silicon.

Broadcom is key to those companies building their own silicon. Rumors are that Broadcom will be a big part of upcoming AWS silicon. Anthropic, Google, and Broadcom signed a deal for 3.5 GW of custom TPU silicon. Estimates from analysts are for $42 billion of Anthropic alone TPU revenue for Broadcom in 2027. This shows the scale of the opportunity.

Broadcom forecasts significantly more than $100 billion in AI revenue by 2027, with Broadcom having secured the supply chain to achieve this. For perspective, Broadcom is guiding for 2027 AI revenue alone per quarter more than the company's revenue today, showing the scale.

Broadcom Company Overview

Broadcom has a history of growing through acquisitions and intelligent integrations of valuable businesses.

Broadcom has acquired major names and parts, including its CA Technologies, Broadcom, Symantec, and largest acquisition ever, VMware. Broadcom once tried to have a larger acquisition of Qualcomm, a $118 billion acquisition, but it was blocked. With the growth of AI, Broadcom has slowed down its investments and new acquisitions; nothing we're disappointed about.

Through these acquisitions, Broadcom has become a tech conglomerate. The company's above offerings are clearly visible, with networking/connectivity, wireless, mainframe, and virtually every other aspect of enterprise computing you can think of. Infrastructure software is ~40%, and semiconductor solutions are ~60% of the company's revenue, showing a diversified portfolio.

Broadcom Financial Performance

Financially, Broadcom has continued to maintain strong performance.

Broadcom saw $19.3 billion in revenue for the quarter, up 29% YoY, an impressive performance. The company carried that to the bottom line with 34% net income growth, and we expect Broadcom to accelerate into 2027. Annualizing the company's EPS of $6/share puts it at a P/E of ~67x, which seems high but is very reasonable given how fast Broadcom is growing.

Broadcom's annualized FCF of $32 billion is 33% YoY growth, a ~2% FCF yield. That enables the company to continue its modest dividend of .7% that it's aggressively grown. Broadcom has used a number of share repurchase programs in the past, and we'd like to see it accelerate this past the $10 billion authorized for the year.

Broadcom is guiding for continued growth in the 2nd quarter with ~$22 billion in revenue and 68% adjusted EBITDA margins. That's almost $3 billion in QoQ revenue growth, and it represents a slight expansion in EBITDA margins from ~67.5%. This impressive project shows the massive ramping demand for Broadcom's AI assets and doesn't count new contracts.

Broadcom Shareholder Returns

Broadcom has substantially grown its dividend returns, although outsized share price growth has kept its yield low.

Broadcom grew its dividend 30% annualized from FY'16 to FY'26 over a 10 year period to $2.6/share annualized. That's a ~0.7% yield on the company's share price. Broadcom has the FCF to comfortably cover this dividend and should be able to continue growing this going forward. Combined with repurchases, Broadcom has numerous paths to shareholder returns.

It's worth noting a mixture of acquisitions and employee compensation has pushed up their outstanding shares. So we'd like to see Broadcom set themselves up so that this number stops growing.

The key to Broadcom's success is the enormous opportunity in AI. The company earned $20 billion in AI revenue in 2025 off of $44 billion in total revenue. Based on the $100 billion forecast for 2027, that implies $150 billion in total revenue for 2027. That 2027 AI forecast is based on 10 GW of 2027 AI demand. Note that this was before expanded deals like Anthropic.

$150 billion in revenue for 2027 is enough for $57 billion in net income (with no margin expansion) or a P/E of less than 31x already within 2 years. That doesn't count expected growth from that level. That enables even stronger shareholder returns going forward.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to the thesis is Broadcom's lofty valuation, which is predicated on the growth of custom AI silicon. That's money only from a handful of companies paying $100+ billion per year on capex for growth. Assuming the customer demand and revenue is not as high as forecast, that could substantially hurt Broadcom's ability to grow into its valuation.

Conclusion

Broadcom has an impressive portfolio of assets. The company is almost a conglomerate of large tech assets, with quality assets like VMware that it recently added to its portfolio. That resulted in Broadcom climbing to its current valuation right around the $2 trillion mark. It's a lofty valuation that Broadcom has to grow into.

There's no guarantee that Broadcom will be able to do so; however, the company is already the de facto silicon partner for a number of cutting edge AI firms, including Anthropic and Google. Continued strength here is key to Broadcom driving its shareholder returns, and we're optimistic given the amount of capital being deployed. That makes Broadcom a valuable investment.

data.description:

data.title: This Week's Market Wrap: Strong Earnings, Ceasefire Uncertainty, And Creeping Inflation
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava

Earnings Run the Tape

Earnings season took center stage this week, but unlike prior quarters where beats alone were enough, this market demanded clarity on margins, capital spending, and forward guidance. The result was a highly fragmented response where individual company narratives, not just sectors, drove performance dispersion.

The defining moment came late in the week with Intel’s (INTC) blowout earnings report, which sent shares surging more than 20% and catalyzed a sharp rally across semiconductors. The Philadelphia Semiconductor Index climbed aggressively, extending its monthly gain to nearly 40%, underscoring just how levered the market remains to AI infrastructure.

Intel’s strength wasn’t isolated; it was reinforced by Texas Instruments (TXN), which posted a standout quarter with data center revenue growth of 90% year-over-year and a strong forward guide. That combination, legacy chipmakers showing credible AI exposure, helped validate broader semiconductor valuations.

On the other side of the ledger, software and select industrial names struggled despite often beating headline estimates. ServiceNow (NOW) fell sharply after its report, with investors questioning whether AI-related costs and growth sustainability were beginning to erode margins.

Similarly, IBM (IBM) delivered solid results but was punished for decelerating revenue growth, highlighting a key theme: the market is no longer rewarding backward-looking strength. It is aggressively discounting future growth trajectories.

The same dynamic showed up in industrials. GE Aerospace (GE) posted strong results with 87% order growth and 25% revenue expansion, yet shares declined as investors focused on guidance durability. Meanwhile, United Airlines (UAL) cut its full-year outlook, citing rising fuel costs—an early signal that margin pressure from energy is already filtering into earnings revisions.

Mega-cap leadership remained critical. Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Meta Platforms (META) provided stability, particularly as AI investment narratives continued to strengthen.

Amazon’s expanded partnership with Anthropic (ANTHRO), potentially exceeding $25 billion, reinforced the scale of hyperscaler spending. At the same time, Tesla (TSLA) delivered a beat but traded lower as investors fixated on a $5 billion increase in capex guidance, raising concerns about free cash flow dilution.

Even with strong earnings, capital allocation decisions mattered. Adobe’s (ADBE) $25 billion buyback provided a tailwind for sentiment in software, signaling management confidence amid broader AI disruption fears. Meanwhile, UnitedHealth (UNH) stood out with a clean beat-and-raise quarter, supported by improved medical cost trends and aggressive buybacks.

Investment implication: Earnings are reinforcing a market bifurcation where capital flows toward companies with clear AI-linked revenue acceleration and away from those facing margin compression or elevated capital spending uncertainty.

Oil Moves Everything

Geopolitics once again proved to be a primary driver of short-term market direction, with the U.S.–Iran situation acting as the central catalyst. The narrative directly influenced intraday volatility, sector rotation, and macro expectations.

The week began with renewed tension after reports that U.S.–Iran talks were faltering, pushing crude oil sharply higher. Prices surged more than 6% in a single session early in the week, closing near $89 per barrel, before continuing higher as uncertainty escalated.

By Tuesday, the situation had deteriorated further. Iran signaled it may not attend negotiations unless U.S. threats were removed, while Vice President JD Vance canceled a planned diplomatic trip. Oil climbed again toward $92 per barrel, triggering a broad market selloff and highlighting the sensitivity of equities to energy shocks.

Midweek brought temporary relief. President Trump extended the ceasefire, allowing more time for negotiations, which helped equities push to record highs. However, oil continued climbing, reaching above $93 per barrel, reflecting persistent skepticism about a durable resolution and underscoring the structural importance of the Strait of Hormuz as a global chokepoint.

The market’s reaction function became clear: equities rallied on diplomatic progress and sold off on escalation headlines. This was particularly evident Thursday, when false reports of Iranian leadership instability triggered a sharp but short-lived selloff before being debunked.

By Friday, sentiment improved again as reports suggested renewed talks in Pakistan, sending oil prices lower from near $98 to the mid-$90s. That decline supported equity strength, particularly in growth and technology names.

The second-order effects were equally important. Rising oil prices pressured airlines, with KLM canceling flights due to fuel costs and United Airlines lowering guidance, while energy companies like Halliburton (HAL) benefited from higher crude. Meanwhile, inflation concerns tied to energy fed directly into bond yields and equity valuations.

Investment implication: Geopolitical volatility, particularly through its impact on energy prices, is acting as a transmission mechanism into inflation expectations, corporate margins, and equity valuations – though we haven’t seen the evidence of inflation pushing through to other wholesale as evidenced by the Producer Price Index last week.

Strong Data, Weak Signal

Economic data this week presented a paradox: strong headline numbers paired with underlying concerns about inflation and real demand. The most notable release was March retail sales, which rose 1.7%, well above expectations.

On the surface, this suggested robust consumer activity. However, a closer look revealed that much of the increase was driven by higher gasoline prices and broader price inflation rather than volume growth. Excluding gasoline, retail sales rose just 0.6%, indicating more modest real demand.

This distinction is critical. Nominal strength driven by price increases does not carry the same economic signal as volume-driven growth. Instead, it points to inflationary pressure eroding purchasing power, which can eventually weigh on consumption.

Labor market data provided some stability. Initial jobless claims rose modestly to 214,000, but remained at levels consistent with a healthy labor market. There were no signs of systemic weakness, reinforcing the idea that the economy is maintaining a slow growth pace rather than deteriorating rapidly.

Forward-looking indicators showed mixed momentum. The S&P Global Manufacturing PMI improved to 54.0, while services rebounded back into expansion territory at 51.3. These readings suggest that business activity remains resilient, particularly in goods-producing sectors benefiting from AI and infrastructure demand.

However, consumer sentiment painted a more cautious picture. The University of Michigan index improved slightly to 49.8 but remains near multi-year lows, reflecting persistent concerns about inflation and cost of living pressures.

Housing data offered a modest bright spot, with pending home sales rising 1.5%, but this came alongside rising inventories, suggesting a more balanced, though still constrained, housing market.

Investment implication: Economic data is reinforcing a late-cycle dynamic where growth persists but is increasingly inflation-driven, complicating expectations for interest rates and pressuring valuation multiples, particularly for long-duration assets.

Chart of the Week

Market sentiment can be evaluated through several indicators, one of the more widely followed being the weekly AAII Investor Sentiment Survey. From a contrarian standpoint, the framework is straightforward: periods of elevated bearishness tend to present more attractive entry points, while extreme bullishness often coincides with stretched conditions.

With the S&P 500 showing an elevated RSI and bullish sentiment approaching one-year highs, the current backdrop suggests a more cautious approach. Chasing performance in this environment increases the risk of poor timing, particularly for new allocations.

For longer-term investors, the more favorable setup typically emerges when bearish sentiment reaches elevated levels, as that tends to reflect pessimism already priced into markets and improves the risk-reward profile for new capital deployment.

Source: AAII Investor Sentiment Survey

Bottom Line

This week reinforced three critical market realities. First, earnings are no longer about beating estimates - they are about proving durability in a higher-cost, AI-intensive environment. Second, geopolitics is not just noise; it is actively shaping inflation, sector performance, and market sentiment through energy prices. Third, economic data continues to show resilience, but beneath the surface, inflation remains a persistent headwind.

Markets remain near highs, but the path forward is increasingly dependent on how these three forces - earnings quality, geopolitical risk, and inflation dynamics - interact.

Original Post

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Can Earnings Outweigh Geopolitical Headwinds And Central Bank Decisions?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Global markets find themselves at a crossroads as the week draws to a close, with the narrative shifting between geopolitical caution and corporate optimism. While the US dollar eased slightly on Friday, it remains on track for a weekly gain as traders weigh the potential for a diplomatic resolution to the conflict between the US, Israel, and Iran.

The greenback’s trajectory has been a reflection of the broader market’s "wait-and-see" approach; optimism over a near-term peace deal has provided temporary relief, while the looming threat of prolonged energy disruptions continues to act as a floor for the currency.

The Dollar Index (DXY) dipped 0.11% to 98.71 during Friday's session, yet it is still eyeing a 0.50% gain for the week. This relative strength has kept the euro and sterling under pressure, with the EUR/USD pair trending toward a 0.53% weekly loss despite a modest intraday recovery to $1.1699. Meanwhile, the Japanese yen saw a slight flight-to-safety bid, strengthening to 159.62.

The geopolitical premium is most visible in the energy sector, where Brent and WTI crude have surged 16% and 11%, respectively, this week, their second-largest gains since the onset of hostilities, as the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively paralyzed.

In the equity space, the mood is decidedly more bullish. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq climbed to new intraday records on Friday, propelled by a dual engine of diplomatic hope and tech outperformance.

Reports that Iran’s foreign minister is headed to Islamabad for peace talks, coupled with news that US envoys, including Jared Kushner, are set for Pakistan-mediated negotiations, have offered a glimmer of hope for de-escalation.

This sentiment was further bolstered by a surge in Intel (INTC) shares, which helped the technology sector shrug off the release of DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) latest AI model and lead the broader market higher.

Earnings season is also providing a sturdy foundation for investor confidence. With over 80% of S&P 500 companies beating expectations thus far, the focus now shifts to a high-stakes week ahead. Five of the "Magnificent Seven" megacaps are scheduled to report, representing a significant portion of the index's market cap.

As the S&P 500 and Nasdaq eye their fourth consecutive week of gains, the longest streak since late 2024, the market's resilience will be tested by whether these corporate giants can justify their valuations amidst a backdrop of lingering inflation concerns and a volatile geopolitical landscape.

Markets enter the final week of April facing a familiar, albeit intensifying, conundrum. While the "higher for longer" narrative was the theme of 2025, the spring of 2026 is shaping up to be defined by a "wait and see" stalemate.

As we look toward the week starting April 26, the spotlight is firmly fixed on a trifecta of central bank meetings with the Fed, ECB, and Bank of England, all of whom find themselves caught between sticky energy-led inflation and a fragile global growth outlook.

Central Banks: All Bark and No Bite?

The overarching theme for the coming week is the "balancing problem" facing global policymakers. Geopolitical instability in the Middle East has kept oil prices buoyant, complicating the inflation path just as markets were hoping for a dovish pivot.

The Fed (Wednesday): Jerome Powell is expected to maintain a steady hand in what is scheduled to be his final meeting as Chair. While the US economy continues to show resilience - with 1Q GDP expected to rebound to 2.7% - the core PCE deflator remains a thorn in the side of the FOMC. Expect a "hold" decision, with Powell likely emphasizing that while the labor market risks are skewed to the downside, the inflation fight is far from over.

The ECB & BoE (Thursday): Across the Atlantic, the story is remarkably similar. Both the ECB and the Bank of England are expected to keep rates unchanged. For the ECB, Thursday’s flurry of data (GDP and April inflation) will serve as a reality check.

In the UK, Governor Andrew Bailey faces a market that has recently ignored his attempts to talk down rate hike expectations. The challenge for both will be maintaining a hawkish bias to keep inflation expectations anchored without accidentally triggering a deeper economic downturn.

Asia in Focus: BoJ and China’s PMI

While the West grapples with policy inertia, the Asia-Pacific region is bracing for potential volatility.

  • Bank of Japan (Tuesday): This is the "wild card" of the week. While the consensus leans toward a hold, a surprise hike remains on the table. With Tokyo CPI expected to accelerate and real interest rates deeply negative, the BoJ is under immense pressure to react. Watch the quarterly outlook report for upward revisions to the 2026/27 inflation forecasts.
  • China PMI (Thursday): After a brief foray into expansionary territory, China’s manufacturing PMI is at risk of slipping back into contraction (49.9 expected). Any sign of cooling demand in the world’s second-largest economy could weigh heavily on commodity-linked currencies and broader risk sentiment.

Australia’s Inflation (Wednesday): High oil prices are expected to push Australian CPI toward the 4.6% mark. This hot print could force the RBA’s hand as early as May, putting the "Aussie" dollar in the crosshairs.

Chart of the Week - US Dollar Index (DXY)

From a technical standpoint, DXY is currently testing an inflection point after a sharp recovery from January lows. Having broken below a rising ascending channel, the index has stalled, suggesting a loss of bullish momentum as it hovers around the 100 and 200-day MAs.

Key Technical Observations:

  • Resistance: The 99.56 handle remains the immediate ceiling. A failure to break this level could lead to a deeper pullback. The catalyst may be some form of deal between the US and Iran.
  • Support: Immediate support sits at the confluence of the MA 100/200 (approx. 98.50). A failure to break above the 99.00 level would keep bears in the driver's seat.
  • Indicators: The RSI (bottom) sits at 45.726, showing a bearish lean after the recent "pivot" high.

The trend is currently neutral-to-bearish. Watch for a decisive close below the 97.70 handle to confirm a deeper correction toward 97.00. Conversely, a daily close above 100.61 invalidates the bearish setup.

US Dollar Index (DXY) Daily Chart, April 24, 2025

The primary barometer for risk. A hawkish "hold" from the Fed could see the greenback challenge recent highs, particularly if GDP data surprises to the upside.

Conversely, should the BoJ opt for a hawkish surprise, expect a sharp unwinding of JPY carry trades, which could spark a broader "risk-off" move across equity markets.

The upcoming week is less about what central bankers do and more about what they say they might do in June. With inflation proving stickier than anticipated and growth figures beginning to show the cracks of high-interest rates, the margin for error has never been thinner.

Traders should remain nimble in a week where the data and the rhetoric are likely to pull in opposite directions.

Original Post

data.title: Crude Oil Trades Above $95 Ahead Of Weekend Risk - WTI Technical Analysis
data.description:
By Elior Manier

The weekend is here, and energy markets are holding their breath.

WTI Crude is hovering uncomfortably around the $95 mark as the geopolitical standoff between Washington and Tehran remains incredibly tense.

While Iran has already sent a delegation to Islamabad, they are actively playing hardball and have yet to officially confirm that they will actually sit down and exchange terms with the US representatives.

Despite this diplomatic stalemate, the US is still sending two of its top diplomats to Pakistan with Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner.

Market participants will be watching the wire like hawks, desperately wanting to see concrete progress toward a finalized peace deal this weekend to avoid a massive gap on Monday's open.

Odds for a peace deal went down quite aggressively since last Friday, and that doesn't bode well for general Market sentiment.

In the meantime, the Trump administration is still applying pressure on Iran to force a deal. The US Navy is maintaining a heavy maritime blockade on the Strait of Hormuz to completely prevent Iran from exploiting its geographic monopoly over the region.

The blockade is in full force, and the US military has blocked 34 tankers coming from Iranian ports, severely tightening the economic chokehold on Tehran.

While global oil traffic is slowly redirecting toward North America—which is currently driving a still increasing $11 per barrel difference between WTI and Brent—global supply is still taking a severe hit.

With alternative pipelines still in the slow process of reopening, the International Energy Agency notes that the market is currently facing a still huge 13 million barrels per day drought compared to normal global flows.

While slightly better economic fundamentals originally helped cool the extreme war premium, it turns out that the overarching narrative remains heavily blurry.

Traders are still wondering exactly where to look, which is directly translating into a highly choppy and seesawing price action.

As the situation has little odds to get more clear ahead of the weekend, let's dive into a multi-timeframe analysis of WTI (US) Oil to determine levels of interest and put the odds in the trader's favor to capitalize on the issue.

US Oil Multi-Timeframe Analysis

WTI Daily Chart

After correcting to $83 lows just last Friday, sellers just couldn't maintain the high pressure fundamentals and this led to a 3-day bounce right back below the $100 level.

As long as prices don't exceed that threshold, Markets should remain contained, but keep a close eye on that level as breaching it could lead to a large spike in volatility.

Let's take a closer look.

WTI 4H Chart and Technical Levels

While sellers reappeared at the $98 to $100 resistance, they could not extend the pressure below the 4H 200-period MA ($96.82).

The moving average is a key indicator to keep your eyes on to gauge intraday momentum in the commodity.

WTI Technical Levels:

Resistance Levels

  • $98 to $100 Resistance (freshly rejected)
  • $104 next-mini resistance
  • $106 to $108 June 2022 Resistance
  • 2022 and Monday highs $117 to $120 (larger channel top)
  • Ukraine War Spike $120 to $124

Support Levels

  • 4H 200-period MA $96.82
  • War Support $93.00 - $95
  • $87 to $90 mini-support (recent bounce)
  • $82.80 to $84 micro-Support
  • 2025 Highs Key Support $78 to $80
  • $69 to $70 Final War Support

1H Chart and action levels

While pre-weekend action brought bullish pressure to the upside, having broken the weekly upward trendline hints at a more balanced action ahead.

Breaking $100 should see follow-through all the way to $104, and the next step is at $106.

On the other hand, rejecting below $97 marks higher chances of a $93 retest.

Expect high-volatility gaps on Monday's session – Trading these could require savvy stop-orders. With heavy binary risk, make sure to keep your size under control.

Safe trades and an enjoyable weekend!

Original Post

data.title: Bulls Charge Back
data.description:
The S&P 500 has finally returned to record highs in the past couple of weeks, and sentiment has reflected that positive price action.

This week’s release of the American Association of Individual Investors survey came in with 46% of respondents reporting bullish sentiment. That is the highest level of bullishness since January 15, 2026.

That surge in bullish sentiment corresponded with an 8.4 percentage point decline in bears. At 34.4%, bearish sentiment per this survey is now at the lowest level since the final week of January.

For bullish sentiment, this week's jump was big. As shown below, the 14.3 percentage point increase week-over-week was the largest one-week uptick since January 23, 2025. Additionally, for the full history of the survey dating back to 1987, this weekly change ranks in the 97th percentile of readings.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Prediction Markets: The Potential Multi‑Trillion Dollar Asset Class Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
By Nicholas Grous, Director of Research, Consumer Internet and Fintech | Varshika Prasanna, Research Analyst, Fintech | Raye Hadi, Research Associate, Digital Assets

In a world where trust in information is breaking down, prediction markets offer a model for truth discovery backed by capital, not opinion. Beyond their early traction in sports, we believe prediction markets’ real potential will come from their instantiation as the future of financial infrastructure, presenting a potential medium-term opportunity up to ~$5 trillion.

Truth Has A Trust Problem… Enter Prediction Markets

In the digital era, information has become the world’s most important asset, as it shapes markets, politics, and individual decisions. Despite unprecedented access to data and analysis, trust in traditional information intermediaries is collapsing. Data from the Pew Research Center suggests that, in the US, adults’ trust in information from national news organizations is near all-time lows, as shown below.

The erosion of trust extends beyond journalism. Confidence in government has been near historic lows for more than 15 years, as only 22% of Americans report that they trust the Federal government to do the right thing most or all of the time, as shown below.2

Increasingly, the general public questions not only the accuracy of information but also the incentives and biases that shape it. Enter prediction markets.

Prediction markets give anyone the opportunity to trade on the potential outcome of future events by assigning prices to uncertainty. A contract that pays one dollar if an event occurs and is trading at sixty-five cents implies that the market is estimating the probability of the outcome at sixty-five percent. Unlike polls or editorial commentary, real capital is backing prices that are updated continuously and capture the wisdom of crowds by aggregating what people believe will happen, not what they say will happen.

The concept of prediction markets is centuries old. In early 16th-century Italy, people used markets to predict who would succeed the next pope, quoting the odds in letters. In 18th-century England, Jonathan’s Coffee House traded on news of parliamentary scandals and changes in prime ministers, eventually becoming the London Stock Exchange.4

In this article, we argue that prediction markets will not disrupt sports betting and that they could become a foundational layer of financial services, impacting markets well beyond sports. As new financial primitives, the most transformational applications associated with prediction markets might not exist today.

Prediction Markets And Sports Betting

Two distinctive types of prediction markets are emerging today, one global and one US-based, with regulation as the key differentiator.

Outside the US, prediction markets are evolving on blockchain-based platforms like Polymarket (POLYMARKET) that are unlocking global liquidity. Founded in 2020 during a murky regulatory environment, Polymarket embraced an open-access design that uses blockchain-based smart contracts to enable permissionless participation in prediction markets. In recent years, Polymarket has tripled its trading volume by scaling its prediction offerings across categories, including politics, sports, and financials, as illustrated below.

In the US, prediction markets hit a regulatory inflection point in 2024 thanks to Kalshi (KALSHI), founded in 2018. First, in 2020, the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) granted Kalshi approval to operate as a Designated Contract Market (DCM) and then, in 2024, it won a landmark legal challenge clearing the way for broad-based event contracts.6

With a regulated framework, Kalshi began paving the way for US prediction markets, which inflected during the presidential election in 2024. As traditional polls pointed to a tight race, Kalshi’s prediction markets signaled a Trump victory throughout the campaign season.

While Kalshi prediction markets span a broad variety of categories, sports contracts have been the standout volume driver since the beginning of the NFL season in September 2025, with weekly notional volume catapulting 21x to dominate trading volume, as shown below.

Does this data suggest that prediction markets are disrupting or displacing online sports betting? Not necessarily. Our research suggests that sports volumes on Kalshi are benefiting from regulatory constraints associated with sports betting in the US that could change over time.

In the US today, 30-32 states representing ~40% of the US population lack access to legal online sports betting (OSB).8 Because the CFTC regulates prediction markets at the federal level, they may not require gambling approval on a state-by-state basis for sports event contracts subject to the ongoing court cases described later in this article. In our view, Kalshi's current sports event contract volume is a function largely of regulatory arbitrage in states which have not legalized online sports betting. According to YipitData, the percentage of Kalshi’s deposit dollars in 2025 from non-legal sports betting states averaged ~60% and was higher during March Madness and the September-December NFL season, as shown below.

Furthermore, based on the ~40% from legal OSB states, deposits on Kalshi are a fraction of those on DraftKings (DKNG), as shown below.

While betting on sports events has driven early engagement in and volume on prediction markets, our research suggests that the activity is a byproduct of regulatory arbitrage. Sports as a percent of total Kalshi volume has dropped from 90% at the end of 2025 to ~75% in March, particularly notable despite March Madness. We acknowledge that multiple state-level cases are moving through US courts concerning whether sports-related event contracts on prediction markets fall under federal CFTC oversight or state gambling laws, including actions in Arizona, Connecticut, Illinois, Nevada, Massachusetts, and others. In that context, a recent Third Circuit decision in New Jersey ruled in favor of Kalshi and held that its sports event contracts are swaps within the CFTC’s exclusive jurisdiction, limiting New Jersey’s ability to regulate those markets as sports betting. In Arizona, a federal judge granted a CFTC request for a temporary restraining order blocking the state from pursuing criminal charges against Kalshi.9 While it is still early days, the regulatory landscape is moving quickly and these rulings could be subject to change. We believe that the endgame for prediction markets is much larger than sports. Longer term, we believe the largest opportunities in prediction markets are likely to surface in financial, economic, and political contracts that offer retail investors a novel way to gain direct exposure to a broad spectrum of real-world outcomes. That opportunity could scale to a size rivaling the derivatives and futures markets.

Prediction Markets As The Future Of Financial Infrastructure

The Real Disruption

According to financial market history, unexpected innovation often surfaces in new markets. In the early 1980s, volatile chicken feed prices made large-scale poultry production risky. Ray Dalio helped McDonald’s hedge its exposure to that risk by using corn and soybean futures, giving the company confidence to launch Chicken McNuggets nationwide.10 What looked like a menu innovation on the surface was a breakthrough in financial engineering. Similarly, prediction markets are introducing new forms of risk management and coordination as retail investors price and manage uncertainty.

Traditional financial markets give institutional investors exposure to or hedges against specific events. Derivatives offer second order exposure, as they bundle risks that might influence the price of a security.

During a technology revolution, however, long-term vision and narratives can influence asset prices as much as near-term fundamentals. In this environment, offering direct exposure to unbundled, discrete outcomes could be game-changing, especially for retail investors.

A fictional Tesla (TSLA) call or put option11 illustrates this point, as shown below.

As a multi-layered instrument, the derivative contract is a composite of the Greeks—which capture how the options respond to price, time, and volatility—the price of the underlying asset, and the myriad catalysts driving the price up and down. Consider a Tesla option. While the underlying is Tesla’s stock, the price is pulled in many directions by disparate forces, including interest rates, quarterly delivery milestones, EV competitor pricing, Elon Musk’s social media activity, and new business opportunities like Robotaxi or Optimus humanoid robots.

Those forces give rise to a dilemmatic bundle of risk. An investor’s thesis may center on a single catalyst, such as the long-term upside of the Optimus humanoid robot, but because the option is tied to TSLA’s stock price, that conviction must absorb the full weight of unrelated volatility, from macro noise to second-order exposures. Traditional financial instruments cannot isolate the impact of such discrete value drivers.

Prediction markets offer a solution. Enabling users to take positions on binary outcomes tied directly to underlying events—“Will Tesla Optimus robots be released by Dec 31, 2026?”—prediction markets unbundle risk and provide direct exposure to specific catalysts or signals. Moreover, event contracts are easier and more intuitive tools for retail investors than are complex derivative contracts that appeal to highly sophisticated investors.

Recognizing Signal

As real-world outcomes become tradable through event contracts, we believe prediction markets are positioned for widespread adoption, but their scaling will require the liquidity that ensures market prices reflect accurately the collective wisdom of participants.

High liquidity produces a strong signal, while lower liquidity amplifies the noise emitted from uninformed or marginal traders, thereby reducing the precision. Importantly, liquidity takes different forms depending on the structure and regulatory context of the market.

The majority of prediction market liquidity is concentrated on Polymarket and Kalshi. Despite their dominance, the liquidity models these platforms employ differ meaningfully, based on divergences in infrastructure, regulatory positioning, and target markets. At their core, prediction market platforms are shaped by their technical architecture and licenses, which in turn influence both the initial liquidity mechanisms and the way they evolve as the platform scales.

The breakdown of the operating structure for some of the most notable prediction market platforms is outlined below. While no structure appears inherently superior in aggregating liquidity, a clear relationship exists between a platform’s operational design and the liquidity strategies best suited to it.

Prediction markets in the US are highly regulated, requiring infrastructure to enable operational control and strict compliance. Like traditional financial markets, prediction markets rely on institutional participation to quote odds, absorb flow, and otherwise maintain liquid markets. Professional market makers (MMs) will be essential to scaling these markets, especially for complex or multi-leg contracts. Robinhood (HOOD) and Coinbase (COIN) already have partnerships with Kalshi, testament to its strong regulatory adherence and MM-centric model that are helping scale liquidity under current US regulation.

Outside the US, regulatory ambiguity has allowed blockchain-based, community-driven platforms like Polymarket to flourish. Leaning on smart contracts and passive central limit order books (CLOBs), its liquidity is driven organically, based primarily on retail activity and peer-to-peer flows. Still, professional MMs serve as complementary liquidity providers.

Traditional platforms rely more on institutional liquidity, while on-chain platforms emphasize community-sourced liquidity through CLOBs and incentives. CLOBs surface liquidity when traders concentrate interest. In contrast, MMs actively shape liquidity, strategically allocating resources based on expected volume, spread opportunities, or market relevance. Incentive programs give permissionless platforms a layer of indirect control, helping to bootstrap markets and guide liquidity without direct intervention and creating a middle ground between organic growth and centralized coordination.

Our research suggests that signal quality improves with liquidity. Liquidity filters out noise and enables market participants to identify meaningful signals. Markets with higher open interest and trading volume incorporate information from a larger and more diverse set of participants, resulting in price dynamics that reflect the “wisdom of the crowds.” In this way, liquidity becomes a proxy for relevance, highlighting the outcomes most important to participants.

During a week when a company is scheduled to report earnings and the Federal Open Markets Committee (FOMC)13 will announce a rate decision, for example, liquidity in related prediction markets can signal which event is more important in moving a stock. As trust in traditional forecasting institutions declines, demand for signal-rich, crowd-sourced pricing should grow. In other words, liquidity not only facilitates trading but also enables truth discovery.

The Opportunity

Prediction markets offer retail investors not only a novel way to price risk by unbundling and isolating individual events but also an efficient alternative to traditional forecasting tools and the bundled exposures found in traditional derivatives markets.

In 2025, prediction markets entered the mainstream. Sports-related volumes grew ~7x, and non-sports-related notional volumes grew ~2.5x. Prediction market volumes in 2026 continue to remain encouraging; if the same run rate for the first quarter continues through the year, sports event contracts will grow ~5x and non-sports event contracts will grow ~6X, highlighting product-market-fit for financial instrument use cases, as shown below.

To estimate the potential market size, our benchmark was global over-the-counter (OTC) derivative volumes, the vast majority of which are driven by institutional activity. As prediction markets mature and begin to resemble established financial instruments like derivatives and futures in both structure and utility, we expect a similar institutional-grade ecosystem to emerge, though meaningful institutional participation remains difficult to gauge and ultimately will be the most significant driver of volume at scale. We model market size across two dimensions: the share of global OTC derivative volume that becomes addressable to prediction markets (25% to 100%) and the adoption rate within that pool (0.5% to 2.5%).

Instead of anchoring a single estimate, the sensitivity table captures the wide band of plausible outcomes depending on how quickly the market develops. At the conservative end, 0.5% adoption against a 25% total addressable market (TAM) implies a market of approximately $1.1 trillion, while at the higher end, 2.5% adoption against a 100% TAM points to approximately $22.6 trillion—a scenario that would require meaningful institutional participation to materialize.

We intentionally frame this as an open forecast, rather than a set end year, given the inherent difficulty in predicting the pace of regulatory evolution, platform development, and mainstream awareness, all of which will shape the adoption curve meaningfully. As those tailwinds accelerate and institutional players begin entering the space, our TAM assumptions could shift materially toward the right columns of the table, representing a significant step-change in total volume potential. We therefore size the potential medium-term opportunity at approximately $1 trillion to $5 trillion, anchoring to the lower end of the sensitivity range as a grounded starting point for a market still in its early stages of development, with a 3-5-year horizon.

Prediction Markets Trading Volume TAM In Trillions

% Of Global Derivative Trading Volume As Addressable Market
25% 50% 75% 100%
% Of Actual Adoption 0.5% 1.1 2.3 3.4 4.5
1% 2.3 4.5 6.8 9.0
1.5% 3.4 6.8 10.2 13.6
2% 4.5 9.0 13.6 18.1
2.5% 5.7 11.3 17.0 22.6

Source: ARK Investment Management LLC, 2026, based on data from Agarwal and Dikshit 2025, International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) 2025.[15] For informational purposes only and should not be considered investment advice or a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any particular security.

Footnotes

  1. Eddy, K. and E. Shearer. 2025. “How Americans’ trust in information from news organizations and social media sites has changed over time.” Pew Research Center.

  2. Pew Research Center. 2025. “Public Trust in Government: 1958-2025.”

  3. Ibid.

  4. Domer. 2025. “A brief history of prediction markets: from papal elections to Polymarket.” OPA News.

  5. Dune Analytics. 2025. “Polymarket Weekly Notional Volume by Category.”

  6. Kalshi. 2020. “Kalshi Wins CFTC Approval for Exchange to Trade Event Contracts.”

  7. Dune Analytics. 2025. “Polymarket Weekly Notional Volume by Category.”

  8. Pempus, B. 2025. “DraftKings Launches Sports ‘Predictions’ Gambling Product.” GamblingHarm.org.

  9. Reuters. 2026. “US judge blocks Arizona criminal case against Kalshi at CFTC's request.” See also Horney, B. 2026. “Court Keeps Kalshi’s Sports Contracts Live in New Jersey.” Front Office Sports.

  10. Vasipenok. V. 2025. “A Classic Tale of Analytical Might: How Ray Dalio Saved Chicken McNuggets.” Quasa.

  11. A “put” option gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to sell a stock at the exercise price. A “call” option gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy a stock at the exercise price.

  12. Saini, M. 2025. “Coinbase doubles down on prediction markets with deal for The Clearing Company.” Reuters. Branzburg, M. 2025. “Coinbase to acquire The Clearing Company: Powering the future of prediction markets.” Coinbase. Finance Magnates. 2026. “Why Robinhood Can't Ditch Kalshi (Yet) Despite Owning Its Own Exchange.”

  13. The FOMC is a branch of the Federal Reserve System that governs the country’s monetary policy, including the Fed Funds Rate, which is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks and credit unions) lend reserve balances to other depository institutions overnight on an uncollateralized basis.

  14. The Block. 2025. “Polymarket, Polymarket US and Kalshi Volume (Monthly).” Dune Analytics. 2025. “Polymarket Weekly Notional Volume by Category.”

  15. International Swaps and Derivatives Association (ISDA) 2025. “Key Trends in the Size and Composition of OTC Derivatives Markets in the Second Half of 2024.” Agarwal, P. and T. Dikshit. 2025. “Retail investors to have more sway over Wall Street after record year.” Reuters.

data.title: GVAL: An Interesting Alternative For Global Value
data.description:
The Cambria Global Value ETF (GVAL) is a relatively unknown fund that may deserve more attention. The fund has only $582.3 million in assets under management even though it has been trading in the market for over 12 years. Yet, last year, GVAL produced a return of almost 56%, not including dividends. Many international funds certainly enjoyed strong results in 2025, but the approach taken by the Cambria Global Value ETF is somewhat unique in that it selects stocks from both developed and emerging markets.

In this analysis, I will explain why I think the fund may be worth holding in an account that is looking for added exposure to international stocks that may be missed by larger, more traditional passively-managed ETFs. The gains from 2025 certainly should not be considered typical, as international funds trail their US counterparts over longer periods of time. Nonetheless, the 13.5% gain posted by GVAL year-to-date shows that the momentum built from last year’s holdings is not ready to fade just yet.

ETF Overview

The Cambria Global Value ETF follows an interesting strategy to select its portfolio. GVAL, like all Cambria funds, is actively managed. The fund’s fact sheet starts with tales of Benjamin Graham and David Dodd in explaining how their holdings are selected. GVAL selects the top 25% cheapest stock markets from a pool of 45 countries using a cyclically adjusted PE ratio. From there, the fund managers use metrics like price-to-book, price-to-sales, price-to-cash flow, and EV/EBITDA to choose the 10 most undervalued stocks of the top 30 largest companies from each of those countries.

The result is a portfolio that looks cheaper on paper than even the typical foreign value ETF. As of April 24, there were 120 stocks held by GVAL. As of March 31, the most recent update from Cambria, the stocks included in the fund have an average PE ratio of 13.1 and trade almost equal to their book value.

As might be expected for a value-oriented fund, the largest sector allocation for the Cambria Global Value ETF is financials at 29.1%. Materials is second at 13.5%, and utilities and energy stocks are the only other two sectors with a weight of at least 10%. The fund is relatively well diversified, with only the health care sector completely unrepresented by its holdings.

The country allocation shown above is certainly notable considering that each one holds a relatively equal share in the fund. Columbia was at the top of the list as of March 31 at 11.1%. Singapore was second at 9.8%. I have often looked for ETFs that had meaningful exposure to Poland besides the iShares MSCI Poland ETF (EPOL), and GVAL fits that description with a 9.6% weight toward that European market. A report last year from Oxford Economics showed that Poland was the home for 10 of the 20 fastest growing cities in all of Europe. While most of the countries on the list would be considered emerging markets, two developed economies, Austria and Great Britain, are included in the top five. Unlike passive funds that follow a specific index and are often rebalanced on a quarterly schedule, the holdings for GVAL can change at any time during the year.

Normally I would go into more detail about the top 10 holdings for an ETF that I was analyzing, but few of the stocks held by GVAL on the list below would be considered household names. At the top of the chart with a weight of 3.65%, AT & S Austria Technologie & Systemtechnik (ASAAF) is a tech company that produces and distributes electronic components. The stock has already gained almost 200% since January 1 of this year. Second on the list is Moneta Money Bank (GMYYF), a diversified bank based in Prague. The only other holding with an allocation greater than 2% of the fund is Komercni Banka, another Czech-based financial stock. KGHM Polska Miedz (KGHPF), fifth on the top 10 holdings, is a Polish mining company that has seen triple digit gains over the last year as metal prices have risen significantly.

GVAL distributes dividends on a quarterly timetable, but there have been three quarters in the last two years when there were no distributions to be made. The total annual payouts decreased in both 2024 and 2025. The fund currently has a yield of 2.86% over the last 12 months.

Analysis of GVAL

Since its inception in 2014, GVAL has only produced an annualized return of 5.92%. Cambria uses the MSCI ACWI Index as a comparison for the fund’s returns, and that index has gained 10.0% during the same time period. However, it is the recent performance of GVAL that is much more noteworthy.

According to Morningstar, the Cambria Global Value ETF has been one of the very best funds in its category over the last month, one year, three years, and five years. The fund is in the top 10% of returns in each of those periods when compared to over 300 peer funds. Even over the last decade, GVAL has been able to produce double-digit annual returns and finish in the top 20% of similar funds.

In the middle of this strong run, there have been some disappointing single years. In 2020, the fund lost over 8% of its value when the average fund in its category produced a small gain. GVAL also finished in the bottom quartile for returns in 2019 and 2021. I think this simply shows the cyclicality for the holdings in a fund like this, where investors can become enamored with emerging markets for a period of time before casting them aside for just as long. The overall returns from the fund over the last 10 years lend more validity to the stock selection methods employed by the fund managers for this ETF.

The expenses for GVAL are slightly below what would be expected from an actively managed international stock fund but above the median for all ETFs. Cambria charges its investors a management fee of 0.59% and an added custodial expense of 0.07% for a total expense ratio of 0.66%. This is certainly not out of line, especially considering the fund's recent returns. In some of its fund literature, Cambria gives data from Morningstar that says this ETF actually compares quite favorably with other foreign value funds that have expense ratios closer to 1%.

Cambria also makes the case that the stocks held by its Global Value ETF are a compelling investment because of their discount to the MSCI ACWI Growth Index. Of course, the stocks in GVAL should always trade at a discount to a growth portfolio, as that is what makes them value stocks, but the gap in valuation since the start of the COVID-19 pandemic has been wider than it was previously. It is worth noting, however, that the discount experienced over the last several years appears to be trending toward an end as we move through 2026.

Risks to Consider

The attraction of a typical value fund is the reduced risk of volatility and significant losses. The Cambria Global Value ETF may not always display the same advantages because a majority of its current holdings are from emerging economies. Countries like Colombia, Singapore, and Thailand give the fund greater exposure to potential political instability, currency fluctuations, and eroding investor confidence. The impact of the current conflict in the Middle East may be greater in emerging markets than in a more developed country that has more options for trade and energy resources. Trade conflicts and logistical congestion could impact these countries’ ability to export their goods and services, decreasing potential growth in their GDP. The lack of liquidity in some emerging markets may also make trading their stocks more expensive and more challenging when trying to adapt to changing conditions in the local economy.

The active management style that is followed by GVAL is susceptible to greater risk as well. There is no guarantee that future returns will look anything like what occurred in 2025. The fund compares itself to the MSCI ACWI Index, which has over 2500 constituents. The effect of just a few bad selections by GVAL’s fund managers could have a greater impact on the fund’s overall returns. The challenges for actively managed funds have been debated for some time, with most all failing to outperform their benchmarks over longer periods.

The Bottom Line

The Cambria Global Value ETF currently has a Buy rating from Seeking Alpha’s Quant analysis. The fund gets high marks for momentum, which should be no surprise given its recent returns, and for risk. The risk score is impacted by the fund’s diversification outside its top 10 holdings as well as its surprisingly low turnover rate from an actively managed ETF. The expense ratio of 0.66% is one of its biggest detractors.

I am not quite as optimistic as SA’s quant rating simply because of the current volatility in world markets and the heightened risks presented by some of the developing countries that are represented in the fund. It is hard to deny GVAL’s track record over the last five quarters. The fund’s portfolio has shown a great deal of resiliency over the last couple of months.

The Cambria Global Value ETF has a four-star rating from Morningstar for its risk-adjusted returns, which is the same as other, better-known international ETFs like the Schwab International Equity ETF (SCHF) and the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund ETF (VEA). By calling GVAL a hold, I am not intending to be overly pessimistic about its future returns, but I think the added risks of emerging markets here make the fund less attractive for a more conservative investor like myself.

data.title: Covenant Logistics Did Not Share The Improvement In The Market In Q1 2026
data.description:
Covenant Logistics (CVLG) released its Q1 2026 results and provided a very interesting data point to analyze what might be the first quarter of recovery in a long time in the trucking industry.

The company’s results did not show improvement, something that surprised me, given that the industry (as shown, for example, on the CASS index) is already improving.

Management said this was mostly because the winter storms of January and February, plus fuel cost headwinds, offset the rate gains and better volumes that appeared in March and April (already in Q2). We will see if this improves in the coming quarters.

On a longer-term basis, I continue to believe CVLG has not shown great capital allocation and that the stock is already pricing a full recovery in capital returns, and then some more. That does not feel coherent with the cyclical nature of the industry, nor with the company’s historically low returns on assets. For this reason, I maintain a Hold on CVLG.

Q1 2026 results

I was excited to read CVLG’s results because the trucking market has started to show some recovery in rates, as shown by indicators like the Cass index. Shipments are still weak across the board, but rates are climbing, and these are generally the first cyclical indicators to improve, particularly when there is supply tightness.

In CVLG’s case, management pointed to a similar dynamic in the call. The company said the quarter included two of the worst months (January and February, because of the winter storms) and one of the best months it had experienced in the past three years, with positive momentum continuing into April. Management also said it was seeing the effect of declining driver and truck capacity, improving demand in some segments and geographies (emphasis on some only), and a stronger pipeline for committed truckload capacity from its customers in the Dedicated segment, signaling tightness in those pockets of the market.

Unfortunately, the recovery is not yet evident in the company's financials.

Freight revenue increased, mainly because of the acquisition of a managed freight services company in Q4 2025, but adjusted operating income declined, indicating margins remain very pressured.

The main problematic segment was Expedited. This is the company’s more operationally leveraged truckload segment, because it requires high utilization to cover fixed costs. Many of the trucks carry two drivers, meaning high labor costs.

In the quarter, expedited freight revenue fell 10%, with adjusted segment operating income collapsing to breakeven from close to $5 million a year ago. The problem came from lower utilization (miles per tractor) because of the harsh winter, the company said.

This dynamic is unfortunately the opposite of what one would want to see in an early-cycle recovery. The company did get a rate improvement, but utilization was too low, particularly in January and February. We will need to see if in Q2 they can maintain rates and grow utilization.

Dedicated looked better, but this is basically optical because of an easy comp. Dedicated freight revenue increased 11%, and operating income tripled to ~$4.5 million. The problem is that last year’s comparison was depressed by avian influenza in the company’s poultry fleet, one of its most important verticals, so the improvement does not necessarily indicate a normal cyclical inflection in returns.

An indication of what management believes about the cycle is that the company continues to sell capacity by selling more trucks than it buys. Average tractor age increased to 26 months from 20 months a year ago.

This might indicate that management is still wary that this is not a complete recovery, or also that the company needs to delever and therefore is 'forced' to sell assets at a cyclical bottom. Net indebtedness fell by ~$50 million in the quarter. If this were the case, it would be super problematic because a cyclical company has to invest during downcycles and divest during upcycles. This is the contrary, a bad sign for future returns across the cycle (important for valuation as we will see below).

The asset-light businesses were also mixed. Managed freight (mainly brokerage) revenue increased 60% YoY, but this came mostly from an acquisition in Q4 2025. Operating income was flat YoY, meaning margins deteriorated. This deterioration came from the impact of higher rates, which generally hurt brokerages when they are fast. Warehousing revenue increased 15% organically, helped by a new customer, but adjusted operating income fell too because it takes time to ramp up profitability in new revenues, according to management.

Overall, the company believes the cycle will improve throughout the year. Management said rate improvements negotiated in Q1 2026 should start to show up in Q2 2026, because new bids take a few weeks to take effect. It also said April was better than March and that Q2 should improve over Q1, with Q3 improving again over Q2.

A related key question is how much of this rate strength will be eaten by other costs. Driver pay is already a topic because part of the upcycle is driven by driver supply tightness (management believes DOT enforcement of driver rules, mainly for foreign drivers entering the US, has already removed 2% to 3% of capacity from the market). Management said drivers are starting to get tight for the first time in about 40 months and that driver pay could rise by mid-single digits, or high single digits if the market gets really hot. It also said driver pay is roughly 30% of the total cost and therefore could absorb 30% to 40% of the first round of rate increases before other inflation items. On top of this, we have costs like new equipment, which management also recognized as more expensive in the call.

On the downside, I agree with management that the main risk is macro. The Iran conflict has already affected fuel and supply-chain conditions, and a prolonged shock could pressure the economy before trucking demand has really recovered. Fuel itself is usually recovered via fuel surcharges, but the second-order effects on freight demand, consumer spending, and supply chains can wreck the supply-driven recovery via a demand-driven crunch.

Valuation

There are two ways to analyze CVLG, or other trucking companies for that matter.

One is speculative, as a vehicle to express a view on the cycle. I have no special view on that. If investors want to play a tightening trucking market, CVLG can work for some time, especially if Expedited finally shows the operating leverage management expects. If the cycle returns to previous peaks, the company will make a lot more money.

The problem is that this speculation becomes a “greater fool” game once the price is already above long-term fair value, and CVLG is already well beyond past ATHs, even from the post-pandemic boom.

From a longer-term perspective, we have to think about returns on capital across the cycle. Last time I analyzed the company, I estimated that CVLG could generate about $55 million of net income across the cycle, based on historical industry cycles, the company’s own returns, and the contribution from TEL (a leasing subsidiary not consolidated in the company's financials).

Today, the company already trades at a market cap of $860 million (25.3 million shares at $34), or roughly 15x that cyclical-average figure.

That does not seem attractive for a capital-intensive, cyclical, relatively commoditized trucking business in a cycle that has not yet fully turned, and with the company needing to cut capacity into the recovery because of leverage.

I do not think the Q1 2026 results change the long-term thesis, although they help flesh out a speculative view on the cycle in trucking. The problem is that the stock price already seems quite ahead of that development.

From all of this, I continue to consider CVLG a Hold.
